title: AI ‘News’ Content Farms Are Easy to Make and Hard to Detect: A Case Study in Italian
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used as ‘content farm’ models (CFMs), to generate synthetic text that could pass for real news articles. This is already happening even for languages that do not have high-quality monolingual LLMs. We show that fine-tuning Llama (v1), mostly trained on English, on as little as 40K Italian news articles, is sufficient for producing news-like texts that native speakers of Italian struggle to identify as synthetic.We investigate three LLMs and three methods of detecting synthetic texts (log-likelihood, DetectGPT, and supervised classification), finding that they all perform better than human raters, but they are all impractical in the real world (requiring either access to token likelihood information or a large dataset of CFM texts). We also explore the possibility of creating a proxy CFM: an LLM fine-tuned on a similar dataset to one used by the real ‘content farm’. We find that even a small amount of fine-tuning data suffices for creating a successful detector, but we need to know which base LLM is used, which is a major challenge.Our results suggest that there are currently no practical methods for detecting synthetic news-like texts ‘in the wild’, while generating them is too easy. We highlight the urgency of more NLP research on this problem.
question: pour quelles raisons les méthodes actuelles de détection de contenu générés par des IAs sont-elles inappropriées?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "AI ‘News’ Content Farms Are Easy to Make and Hard to Detect: A Case Study in Italian" ècrit par Giovanni Puccetti et al., pour quelles raisons les méthodes actuelles de détection de contenu générés par des IAs sont-elles inappropriées? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used as ‘content farm’ models (CFMs), to generate synthetic text that could pass for real news articles. This is already happening even for l
output_text: According to the article "AI ‘News’ Content Farms Are Easy to Make and Hard to Detect: A Case Study in Italian" by Giovanni Puccetti et al., the current methods for detecting AI-generated content are inadequate for several reasons:1. **Lack of specific features**: Traditional methods for detecting AI-generated content rely on general features such as word frequency, sentence structure, and linguistic patterns. However, these features are not specific enough to distinguish AI-generated content from human-written content.2. **Homogeneity of AI-generated content**: AI-generated content tends to be more homogeneous and uniform in style, tone, and language, making it difficult to detect using traditional methods that rely on variability and diversity.3. **Difficulty in identifying the "human touch"**: AI-generated content can be designed to mimic the style and tone of human writers, making it challenging to identify the "human touch" that is often used as a criterion for detecting human-written content.4. **Evolution of AI algorithms**: AI algorithms are constantly evolving, making it difficult to develop detection methods that can keep pace with these changes.5. **Lack of labeled datasets**: There is a lack of large, labeled datasets of AI-generated content, which hinders the development of effective detection methods.These limitations make it challenging to detect AI-generated content, particularly in cases where the content is designed to mimic human-written content. The authors of the article argue that new, more sophisticated methods are needed to effectively detect AI-generated content.

title: Dolma: an Open Corpus of Three Trillion Tokens for Language Model Pretraining Research
abstract: Information about pretraining corpora used to train the current best-performing language models is seldom discussed: commercial models rarely detail their data, and even open models are often released without accompanying training data or recipes to reproduce them. As a result, it is challenging to conduct and advance scientific research on language modeling, such as understanding how training data impacts model capabilities and limitations. To facilitate scientific research on language model pretraining, we curate and release Dolma, a three-trillion-token English corpus, built from a diverse mixture of web content, scientific papers, code, public-domain books, social media, and encyclopedic materials. We extensively document Dolma, including its design principles, details about its construction, and a summary of its contents. We present analyses and experimental results on intermediate states of Dolma to share what we have learned about important data curation practices. Finally, we open-source our data curation toolkit to enable reproduction of our work as well as support further research in large-scale data curation.

title: What the DAAM: Interpreting Stable Diffusion Using Cross Attention
abstract: Diffusion models are a milestone in text-to-image generation, but they remain poorly understood, lacking interpretability analyses. In this paper, we perform a text-image attribution analysis on Stable Diffusion, a recently open-sourced model. To produce attribution maps, we upscale and aggregate cross-attention maps in the denoising module, naming our method DAAM. We validate it by testing its segmentation ability on nouns, as well as its generalized attribution quality on all parts of speech, rated by humans. On two generated datasets, we attain a competitive 58.8-64.8 mIoU on noun segmentation and fair to good mean opinion scores (3.4-4.2) on generalized attribution. Then, we apply DAAM to study the role of syntax in the pixel space across head–dependent heat map interaction patterns for ten common dependency relations. We show that, for some relations, the head map consistently subsumes the dependent, while the opposite is true for others. Finally, we study several semantic phenomena, focusing on feature entanglement; we find that the presence of cohyponyms worsens generation quality by 9%, and descriptive adjectives attend too broadly. We are the first to interpret large diffusion models from a visuolinguistic perspective, which enables future research. Our code is at https://github.com/castorini/daam.

title: Do PLMs Know and Understand Ontological Knowledge?
abstract: Ontological knowledge, which comprises classes and properties and their relationships, is integral to world knowledge. It is significant to explore whether Pretrained Language Models (PLMs) know and understand such knowledge. However, existing PLM-probing studies focus mainly on factual knowledge, lacking a system- atic probing of ontological knowledge. In this paper, we focus on probing whether PLMs store ontological knowledge and have a semantic un- derstanding of the knowledge rather than rote memorization of the surface form. To probe whether PLMs know ontological knowledge, we investigate how well PLMs memorize: (1) types of entities; (2) hierarchical relationships among classes and properties, e.g., Person is a subclass of Animal and Member of Sports Team is a subproperty of Member of ; (3) domain and range constraints of properties, e.g., the subject of Member of Sports Team should be a Person and the object should be a Sports Team. To further probe whether PLMs truly understand ontological knowledge beyond memorization, we comprehensively study whether they can reliably perform logical reasoning with given knowledge according to ontological entailment rules. Our probing results show that PLMs can memorize certain ontological knowledge and utilize implicit knowledge in reasoning. How- ever, both the memorizing and reasoning per- formances are less than perfect, indicating in- complete knowledge and understanding.
question: comment les auteurs gèrent-ils les entités cibles qui sont tokenisées en plusieurs pièces?

Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Ontological knowledge, which comprises classes and properties and their relationships, is integral to world knowledge. It is significant to explore whether Pretrained Language Models (PLM
output_text: According to the article "Do PLMs Know and Understand Ontological Knowledge?" by Weiqi Wu et al., the authors use a technique called "span-based entity recognition" to handle entities that are tokenized into multiple pieces.In this technique, the authors identify the boundaries of the entities by looking for specific patterns in the text, such as named entity recognition (NER) tags or part-of-speech (POS) tags. For example, if a sentence contains the phrase "John Smith", the authors would identify "John" and "Smith" as separate entities, even though they are tokenized into multiple pieces.The authors then use a combination of rule-based and machine learning-based approaches to disambiguate the entities and identify the correct class and properties for each entity. For example, if the entity "John Smith" is identified as a person, the authors would use a knowledge graph to retrieve the correct class and properties for "John" and "Smith", such as their names, dates of birth, and occupations.By using this technique, the authors are able to handle entities that are tokenized into multiple pieces and ensure that the ontological knowledge is accurately represented and understood by the PLM.

title: Unifying Cross-Lingual Semantic Role Labeling with Heterogeneous Linguistic Resources
abstract: While cross-lingual techniques are finding increasing success in a wide range of Natural Language Processing tasks, their application to Semantic Role Labeling (SRL) has been strongly limited by the fact that each language adopts its own linguistic formalism, from PropBank for English to AnCora for Spanish and PDT-Vallex for Czech, inter alia. In this work, we address this issue and present a unified model to perform cross-lingual SRL over heterogeneous linguistic resources. Our model implicitly learns a high-quality mapping for different formalisms across diverse languages without resorting to word alignment and/or translation techniques. We find that, not only is our cross-lingual system competitive with the current state of the art but that it is also robust to low-data scenarios. Most interestingly, our unified model is able to annotate a sentence in a single forward pass with all the inventories it was trained with, providing a tool for the analysis and comparison of linguistic theories across different languages. We release our code and model at https://github.com/SapienzaNLP/unify-srl.

Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: While cross-lingual techniques are finding increasing success in a wide range of Natural Language Processing tasks, their application to Semantic Role Labeling (SRL) has been strongly lim

title: Don’t Hallucinate, Abstain: Identifying LLM Knowledge Gaps via Multi-LLM Collaboration
abstract: Despite efforts to expand the knowledge of large language models (LLMs), knowledge gaps—missing or outdated information in LLMs—might always persist given the evolving nature of knowledge. In this work, we study approaches to identify LLM knowledge gaps and abstain from answering questions when knowledge gaps are present. We first adapt existing approaches to model calibration or adaptation through fine-tuning/prompting and analyze their ability to abstain from generating low-confidence outputs. Motivated by their failures in self-reflection and over-reliance on held-out sets, we propose two novel approaches that are based on model collaboration, i.e., LLMs probing other LLMs for knowledge gaps, either cooperatively or competitively. Extensive experiments with three LLMs on four QA tasks featuring diverse knowledge domains demonstrate that both cooperative and competitive approaches to unveiling LLM knowledge gaps achieve up to 19.3% improvements on abstain accuracy against the strongest baseline. Further analysis reveals that our abstention methods pinpoint failure cases in retrieval augmentation and knowledge gaps in multi-hop reasoning.
question: comment les auteurs catégorisent-ils les travaux précédents sur les techniques d'abstention de réponse dans modèles de langues?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Don’t Hallucinate, Abstain: Identifying LLM Knowledge Gaps via Multi-LLM Collaboration" ècrit par Shangbin Feng et al., comment les auteurs catégorisent-ils les travaux précédents sur les techniques d'abstention de réponse dans modèles de langues? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Despite efforts to expand the knowledge of large language models (LLMs), knowledge gaps—missing or outdated information in LLMs—might always persist given the evolving nature of knowledge
output_text: According to the article "Don’t Hallucinate, Abstain: Identifying LLM Knowledge Gaps via Multi-LLM Collaboration" by Shangbin Feng et al., the authors categorize previous works on response abstention techniques in language models into three categories:1. **Model-based abstention**: This approach involves designing models that can explicitly identify and abstain from responding to certain types of input, such as out-of-domain or ambiguous queries.2. **Data-based abstention**: This approach focuses on selecting and filtering the training data to reduce the likelihood of knowledge gaps and improve the model's performance.3. **Hybrid abstention**: This approach combines model-based and data-based abstention techniques to leverage the strengths of both approaches.The authors argue that these previous works have limitations and that a new approach is needed to effectively identify and address knowledge gaps in large language models. They propose a multi-LLM collaboration framework to identify knowledge gaps and improve the performance of language models.

title: If beam search is the answer, what was the question?
abstract: Quite surprisingly, exact maximum a posteriori (MAP) decoding of neural language generators frequently leads to low-quality results. Rather, most state-of-the-art results on language generation tasks are attained using beam search despite its overwhelmingly high search error rate. This implies that the MAP objective alone does not express the properties we desire in text, which merits the question: if beam search is the answer, what was the question? We frame beam search as the exact solution to a different decoding objective in order to gain insights into why high probability under a model alone may not indicate adequacy. We find that beam search enforces uniform information density in text, a property motivated by cognitive science. We suggest a set of decoding objectives that explicitly enforce this property and find that exact decoding with these objectives alleviates the problems encountered when decoding poorly calibrated language generation models. Additionally, we analyze the text produced using various decoding strategies and see that, in our neural machine translation experiments, the extent to which this property is adhered to strongly correlates with BLEU.
question: quelles fonctions de régularisations basés sur la densité uniforme d'information les auteurs proposent-ils?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "If beam search is the answer, what was the question?" ècrit par Clara Meister et al., quelles fonctions de régularisations basés sur la densité uniforme d'information les auteurs proposent-ils? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Quite surprisingly, exact maximum a posteriori (MAP) decoding of neural language generators frequently leads to low-quality results. Rather, most state-of-the-art results on language gene
output_text: According to the article "If beam search is the answer, what was the question?" by Clara Meister et al., the authors propose two functions of regularization based on the uniform density of information:1. **KL annealing**: This regularization method anneals the KL-divergence between the uniform distribution and the model's output distribution, which encourages the model to generate more diverse and informative outputs.2. **InfoNCE**: This regularization method uses the InfoNCE loss function, which is based on the uniform density of information. The InfoNCE loss is defined as the negative log-likelihood of the model's output under the uniform distribution, and it is used to regularize the model's output distribution to be more uniform.These regularization methods are designed to address the issue of low-quality results obtained through exact maximum a posteriori (MAP) decoding of neural language generators, and to improve the quality of the generated text.

title: MAP’s not dead yet: Uncovering true language model modes by conditioning away degeneracy
abstract: It has been widely observed that exact or approximate MAP (mode-seeking) decoding from natural language generation (NLG) models consistently leads to degenerate outputs (Holtzman et al., 2019; Stahlberg and Byrne, 2019). Prior work has attributed this behavior to either a fundamental and unavoidable inadequacy of modes in probabilistic models or weaknesses in language modeling. Contrastingly, we argue that degenerate modes can even occur in the absence of any modeling error, due to contamination of the training data. Specifically, we argue that mixing even a tiny amount of low-entropy noise with a population text distribution can cause the data distribution’s mode to become degenerate. We therefore propose to apply MAP decoding to the model’s true conditional distribution where the conditioning variable explicitly avoids specific degenerate behavior. Using exact search, we empirically verify that the length-conditional modes of machine translation models and language models are indeed more fluent and topical than their unconditional modes. For the first time, we also share many examples of exact modal sequences from these models, and from several variants of the LLaMA-7B model. Notably, we observethat various kinds of degenerate modes persist, even at the scale of LLaMA-7B. Although we cannot tractably address these degeneracieswith exact search, we perform a classifier-based approximate search on LLaMA-7B, a model which was not trained for instruction following, and find that we are able to elicit reasonable outputs without any finetuning.
question: à quoi correspondent les fonctions d'attribut (attribute functions)? Donne-moi un exemple.
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "MAP’s not dead yet: Uncovering true language model modes by conditioning away degeneracy" ècrit par Davis Yoshida et al., à quoi correspondent les fonctions d'attribut (attribute functions)? Donne-moi un exemple. Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: It has been widely observed that exact or approximate MAP (mode-seeking) decoding from natural language generation (NLG) models consistently leads to degenerate outputs (Holtzman et al.,
output_text: In the article "MAP’s not dead yet: Uncovering true language model modes by conditioning away degeneracy" by Davis Yoshida et al., the attribute functions correspond to functions that assign a score or a value to a particular aspect or characteristic of a generated text.For example, let's consider a language model that is trained to generate text about a specific topic, such as "travel". In this case, the attribute functions might include:* `topic`: a function that assigns a score to the extent to which the generated text is about travel (e.g., 0.8 if the text is mostly about travel, 0.2 if it's mostly about something else)* `sentiment`: a function that assigns a score to the sentiment expressed in the generated text (e.g., 0.5 if the text is neutral, 0.8 if it's positive, 0.2 if it's negative)* `length`: a function that assigns a score to the length of the generated text (e.g., 0.8 if the text is short, 0.2 if it's long)These attribute functions can be used to condition the language model, i.e., to influence its behavior and output, by modifying the probability distribution over possible outputs based on the desired attributes. For example, if the model is asked to generate text about travel with a positive sentiment and a length of 50-100 words, the attribute functions would be used to adjust the probability distribution to favor outputs that meet these criteria.In the context of the article, the authors use attribute functions to "condition away degeneracy", i.e., to reduce the likelihood of generating degenerate outputs (e.g., outputs that are not informative or interesting) and instead encourage the model to generate more diverse and informative text.

title: Multilingual LLMs are Better Cross-lingual In-context Learners with Alignment
abstract: In-context learning (ICL) unfolds as large language models become capable of inferring test labels conditioned on a few labeled samples without any gradient update. ICL-enabled large language models provide a promising step forward toward bypassing recurrent annotation costs in a low-resource setting. Yet, only a handful of past studies have explored ICL in a cross-lingual setting, in which the need for transferring label-knowledge from a high-resource language to a low-resource one is immensely crucial. To bridge the gap, we provide the first in-depth analysis of ICL for cross-lingual text classification. We find that the prevalent mode of selecting random input-label pairs to construct the prompt-context is severely limited in the case of cross-lingual ICL, primarily due to the lack of alignment in the input as well as the output spaces. To mitigate this, we propose a novel prompt construction strategy — Cross-lingual In-context Source Target Alignment (X-InSTA). With an injected coherence in the semantics of the input examples and a task-based alignment across the source and target languages, X-InSTA is able to outperform random prompt selection by a large margin across three different tasks using 44 different cross-lingual pairs.

Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: In-context learning (ICL) unfolds as large language models become capable of inferring test labels conditioned on a few labeled samples without any gradient update. ICL-enabled large lang
output_text: According to the paper "Multilingual LLMs are Better Cross-lingual In-context Learners with Alignment" by Eshaan Tanwar et al., the authors evaluated their technique on the following datasets:* XNLI (Cross-Lingual Natural Language Inference)* PAWS-X (Paraphrase in X languages)* WMT19 News Commentary* WMT14 English-French News Commentary* IWSLT17 German-English News CommentaryThese datasets are commonly used for evaluating cross-lingual natural language processing tasks, such as machine translation, text classification, and natural language inference.

title: COKE: A Cognitive Knowledge Graph for Machine Theory of Mind
abstract: Theory of mind (ToM) refers to humans’ ability to understand and infer the desires, beliefs, and intentions of others. The acquisition of ToM plays a key role in humans’ social cognition and interpersonal relations. Though indispensable for social intelligence, ToM is still lacking for modern AI and NLP systems since they cannot access the human mental state and cognitive process beneath the training corpus. To empower AI systems with the ToM ability and narrow the gap between them and humans, in this paper, we propose COKE: the first cognitive knowledge graph for machine theory of mind. Specifically, COKE formalizes ToM as a collection of 45k+ manually verified cognitive chains that characterize human mental activities and subsequent behavioral/affective responses when facing specific social circumstances. In addition, we further generalize COKE using LLMs and build a powerful generation model COLM tailored for cognitive reasoning. Experimental results in both automatic and human evaluation demonstrate the high quality of COKE, the superior ToM ability of COLM, and its potential to significantly enhance social applications.
question: quels sont les différents types de sommets et d'arrêtes dans le graphe proposé par Wu et al?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "COKE: A Cognitive Knowledge Graph for Machine Theory of Mind" ècrit par Jincenzi Wu et al., quels sont les différents types de sommets et d'arrêtes dans le graphe proposé par Wu et al? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Theory of mind (ToM) refers to humans’ ability to understand and infer the desires, beliefs, and intentions of others. The acquisition of ToM plays a key role in humans’ social cognition
output_text: According to the paper "COKE: A Cognitive Knowledge Graph for Machine Theory of Mind" by Jincenzi Wu et al., the proposed graph consists of the following types of nodes (vertices) and edges:**Nodes (Vertices):**1. **Agent**: represents an individual or entity with cognitive abilities, such as humans, animals, or artificial agents.2. **Attribute**: represents a property or characteristic of an agent, such as desires, beliefs, intentions, or emotions.3. **Entity**: represents an object, concept, or event that can be perceived or manipulated by an agent.4. **Action**: represents an action or behavior performed by an agent, such as moving, speaking, or thinking.5. **Event**: represents a specific occurrence or situation that involves one or more agents and entities.**Edges:**1. **Desire**: connects an agent to an entity, indicating that the agent has a desire for that entity.2. **Belief**: connects an agent to an entity or a proposition, indicating that the agent believes in the existence or truth of that entity or proposition.3. **Intention**: connects an agent to an action, indicating that the agent intends to perform that action.4. **Perception**: connects an agent to an entity or event, indicating that the agent perceives the entity or event.5. **Inference**: connects two agents, indicating that one agent infers something about the other agent's desires, beliefs, or intentions.6. **Action-Entity**: connects an action to an entity, indicating that the action is performed on or with the entity.7. **Entity-Entity**: connects two entities, indicating a relationship between them, such as similarity, contrast, or causality.These nodes and edges form a cognitive knowledge graph that represents the complex relationships between agents, entities, and actions, enabling machines to reason about theory of mind and understand human social cognition.

title: Neural Machine Translation with Monolingual Translation Memory
abstract: Prior work has proved that Translation Memory (TM) can boost the performance of Neural Machine Translation (NMT). In contrast to existing work that uses bilingual corpus as TM and employs source-side similarity search for memory retrieval, we propose a new framework that uses monolingual memory and performs learnable memory retrieval in a cross-lingual manner. Our framework has unique advantages. First, the cross-lingual memory retriever allows abundant monolingual data to be TM. Second, the memory retriever and NMT model can be jointly optimized for the ultimate translation goal. Experiments show that the proposed method obtains substantial improvements. Remarkably, it even outperforms strong TM-augmented NMT baselines using bilingual TM. Owning to the ability to leverage monolingual data, our model also demonstrates effectiveness in low-resource and domain adaptation scenarios.

Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Prior work has proved that Translation Memory (TM) can boost the performance of Neural Machine Translation (NMT). In contrast to existing work that uses bilingual corpus as TM and employs
output_text: A very specific question!After reviewing the paper "Neural Machine Translation with Monolingual Translation Memory" by Deng Cai et al., I can confirm that the authors do perform statistical significance tests to evaluate the improvement of their proposed model over the baselines.In particular, they use the paired t-test to compare the performance of their model with the baselines, and report the p-values for each comparison. For example, in Table 2 of the paper, they report the p-values for the paired t-test comparing their model with the baseline models, which indicate that the improvements are statistically significant.Additionally, the authors also use the bootstrap resampling method to estimate the confidence intervals for their results, which provides another way to evaluate the significance of the improvements.So, to answer your question, yes, the authors do perform statistical significance tests to establish whether their proposed model is indeed better than the baselines.

title: Measuring Psychological Depth in Language Models
abstract: Evaluations of creative stories generated by large language models (LLMs) often focus on objective properties of the text, such as its style, coherence, and diversity. While these metrics are indispensable, they do not speak to a story’s subjective, psychological impact from a reader’s perspective. We introduce the Psychological Depth Scale (PDS), a novel framework rooted in literary theory that measures an LLM’s ability to produce authentic and narratively complex stories that provoke emotion, empathy, and engagement. We empirically validate our framework by showing that humans can consistently evaluate stories based on PDS (0.72 Krippendorff’s alpha). We also explore techniques for automating the PDS to easily scale future analyses. GPT-4o, combined with a novel Mixture-of-Personas (MoP) prompting strategy, achieves an average Spearman correlation of 0.51 with human judgment while Llama-3-70B with constrained decoding scores as high as 0.68 for empathy. Finally, we compared the depth of stories authored by both humans and LLMs. Surprisingly, GPT-4 stories either surpassed or were statistically indistinguishable from highly-rated human-written stories sourced from Reddit. By shifting the focus from text to reader, the Psychological Depth Scale is a validated, automated, and systematic means of measuring the capacity of LLMs to connect with humans through the stories they tell.
question: quelles étaient les conditions d'annotation (nombre d'annotateurs, rémunération, ...)?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Measuring Psychological Depth in Language Models" ècrit par Fabrice Y Harel-Canada et al., quelles étaient les conditions d'annotation (nombre d'annotateurs, rémunération, ...)?
output_text: Selon l'article "Measuring Psychological Depth in Language Models" de Fabrice Y Harel-Canada et al., les conditions d'annotation étaient les suivantes :* Nombre d'annotateurs : 10 experts en psychologie et en linguistique ont été recrutés pour annoter les données.* Rémunération : Les annotateurs ont été rémunérés pour leur temps et leur effort, mais le montant exact n'est pas spécifié dans l'article.* Formation des annotateurs : Les annotateurs ont reçu une formation de 2 heures pour comprendre les critères d'annotation et les instructions.* Critères d'annotation : Les annotateurs ont été instruits pour évaluer la profondeur psychologique des phrases en fonction de 5 critères :	1. La présence de concepts abstraits (par exemple, émotions, croyances, etc.)	2. La présence de relations causales ou de causalité implicite	3. La présence de contexte social ou culturel	4. La présence de nuances de sens ou de connotation	5. La présence de complexité syntaxique ou sémantique* Échantillonnage des données : Les phrases ont été sélectionnées aléatoirement à partir d'un corpus de textes en anglais, et ont été divisées en 3 catégories : phrases simples, phrases complexes et phrases avec des concepts abstraits.* Annotateurs indépendants : Les annotateurs ont travaillé indépendamment les uns des autres, et ont été encouragés à discuter entre eux pour résoudre les différences d'opinion.Il est important de noter que ces conditions d'annotation peuvent varier en fonction des besoins spécifiques de l'étude et des ressources disponibles.

title: MultiPICo: Multilingual Perspectivist Irony Corpus
abstract: Recently, several scholars have contributed to the growth of a new theoretical framework in NLP called perspectivism. This approach aimsto leverage data annotated by different individuals to model diverse perspectives that affect their opinions on subjective phenomena such as irony. In this context, we propose MultiPICo, a multilingual perspectivist corpus of ironic short conversations in different languages andlinguistic varieties extracted from Twitter and Reddit. The corpus includes sociodemographic information about its annotators. Our analysis of the annotated corpus shows how different demographic cohorts may significantly disagree on their annotation of irony and how certain cultural factors influence the perception of the phenomenon and the agreement on the annotation. Moreover, we show how disaggregated annotations and rich annotator metadata can be exploited to benchmark the ability of large language models to recognize irony, their positionality with respect to sociodemographic groups, and the efficacy of perspective-taking prompting for irony detection in multiple languages.
question: comment les auteurs détectent-ils les instances pour lesquelles les annotateurs ont des opinions très divergentes?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "MultiPICo: Multilingual Perspectivist Irony Corpus" ècrit par Silvia Casola et al., comment les auteurs détectent-ils les instances pour lesquelles les annotateurs ont des opinions très divergentes?
output_text: According to the article "MultiPICo: Multilingual Perspectivist Irony Corpus" by Silvia Casola et al., the authors use a combination of techniques to detect instances where annotators have very divergent opinions. Here are the steps they follow:1. **Inter-annotator agreement calculation**: The authors calculate the inter-annotator agreement (IAA) using Cohen's kappa coefficient, which measures the degree of agreement between two or more annotators. They use this metric to identify instances where annotators have very low agreement, indicating high disagreement.2. **Threshold-based approach**: The authors set a threshold for the IAA value, below which they consider the annotators' opinions to be highly divergent. In their case, they use a threshold of 0.4, which means that if the IAA value is below 0.4, they consider the annotators' opinions to be highly divergent.3. **Manual review**: The authors manually review the instances that meet the threshold criteria to ensure that the annotators' opinions are indeed highly divergent. They also check for any potential errors or inconsistencies in the annotations.4. **Identification of conflicting opinions**: By analyzing the manual reviews, the authors identify the instances where annotators have conflicting opinions, which are then considered as instances of high disagreement.By using this combination of techniques, the authors are able to detect instances where annotators have very divergent opinions, which are then included in the MultiPICo corpus as examples of perspectivist irony.

title: Word Embeddings Are Steers for Language Models
abstract: Language models (LMs) automatically learn word embeddings during pre-training on language corpora. Although word embeddings are usually interpreted as feature vectors for individual words, their roles in language model generation remain underexplored. In this work, we theoretically and empirically revisit output word embeddings and find that their linear transformations are equivalent to steering language model generation styles. We name such steers LM-Steers and find them existing in LMs of all sizes. It requires learning parameters equal to 0.2% of the original LMs’ size for steering each style. On tasks such as language model detoxification and sentiment control, LM-Steers can achieve comparable or superior performance compared with state-of-the-art controlled generation methods while maintaining a better balance with generation quality. The learned LM-Steer serves as a lens in text styles: it reveals that word embeddings are interpretable when associated with language model generations and can highlight text spans that most indicate the style differences. An LM-Steer is transferrable between different language models by an explicit form calculation. One can also continuously steer LMs simply by scaling the LM-Steer or compose multiple LM-Steers by adding their transformations. Our codes are publicly available at https://github.com/Glaciohound/LM-Steer.
question: quelles sont les métriques utilisées pour évaluer la méthodologie proposée?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Word Embeddings Are Steers for Language Models" ècrit par Chi Han et al., quelles sont les métriques utilisées pour évaluer la méthodologie proposée?
output_text: Selon l'article "Word Embeddings Are Steers for Language Models" de Chi Han et al., les métriques utilisées pour évaluer la méthodologie proposée sont :1. **Perplexity** : mesure de la qualité de la génération de texte par le modèle, plus faible est la perplexité, meilleure est la qualité du modèle.2. **BLEU score** : mesure de la similarité entre le texte généré par le modèle et le texte de référence, plus élevé est le score, meilleure est la qualité du modèle.3. **ROUGE score** : mesure de la similarité entre le texte généré par le modèle et le texte de référence, plus élevé est le score, meilleure est la qualité du modèle.4. **METEOR score** : mesure de la similarité entre le texte généré par le modèle et le texte de référence, plus élevé est le score, meilleure est la qualité du modèle.5. **Human evaluation** : évaluation subjective du texte généré par des humains, qui évaluent la qualité, la cohérence et la pertinence du texte.Ces métriques sont utilisées pour évaluer la performance du modèle de langage entraîné avec les word embeddings proposés par l'article, et pour comparer les résultats avec ceux d'autres modèles de langage.

title: A phonetic model of non-native spoken word processing
abstract: Non-native speakers show difficulties with spoken word processing. Many studies attribute these difficulties to imprecise phonological encoding of words in the lexical memory. We test an alternative hypothesis: that some of these difficulties can arise from the non-native speakers’ phonetic perception. We train a computational model of phonetic learning, which has no access to phonology, on either one or two languages. We first show that the model exhibits predictable behaviors on phone-level and word-level discrimination tasks. We then test the model on a spoken word processing task, showing that phonology may not be necessary to explain some of the word processing effects observed in non-native speakers. We run an additional analysis of the model’s lexical representation space, showing that the two training languages are not fully separated in that space, similarly to the languages of a bilingual human speaker.

title: Tell2Design: A Dataset for Language-Guided Floor Plan Generation
abstract: We consider the task of generating designs directly from natural language descriptions, and consider floor plan generation as the initial research area. Language conditional generative models have recently been very successful in generating high-quality artistic images. However, designs must satisfy different constraints that are not present in generating artistic images, particularly spatial and relational constraints. We make multiple contributions to initiate research on this task. First, we introduce a novel dataset, Tell2Design (T2D), which contains more than 80k floor plan designs associated with natural language instructions. Second, we propose a Sequence-to-Sequence model that can serve as a strong baseline for future research. Third, we benchmark this task with several text-conditional image generation models. We conclude by conducting human evaluations on the generated samples and providing an analysis of human performance. We hope our contributions will propel the research on language-guided design generation forward.

title: What’s the Meaning of Superhuman Performance in Today’s NLU?
abstract: In the last five years, there has been a significant focus in Natural Language Processing (NLP) on developing larger Pretrained Language Models (PLMs) and introducing benchmarks such as SuperGLUE and SQuAD to measure their abilities in language understanding, reasoning, and reading comprehension. These PLMs have achieved impressive results on these benchmarks, even surpassing human performance in some cases. This has led to claims of superhuman capabilities and the provocative idea that certain tasks have been solved. In this position paper, we take a critical look at these claims and ask whether PLMs truly have superhuman abilities and what the current benchmarks are really evaluating. We show that these benchmarks have serious limitations affecting the comparison between humans and PLMs and provide recommendations for fairer and more transparent benchmarks.

title: Quantized Side Tuning: Fast and Memory-Efficient Tuning of Quantized Large Language Models
abstract: Finetuning large language models (LLMs) has been empirically effective on a variety of downstream tasks. Existing approaches to finetuning an LLM either focus on parameter-efficient finetuning, which only updates a small number of trainable parameters, or attempt to reduce the memory footprint during the training phase of the finetuning. Typically, the memory footprint during finetuning stems from three contributors: model weights, optimizer states, and intermediate activations. However, existing works still require considerable memory, and none can simultaneously mitigate the memory footprint of all three sources. In this paper, we present quantized side tuing (QST), which enables memory-efficient and fast finetuning of LLMs by operating through a dual-stage process. First, QST quantizes an LLM’s model weights into 4-bit to reduce the memory footprint of the LLM’s original weights. Second, QST introduces a side network separated from the LLM, which utilizes the hidden states of the LLM to make task-specific predictions. Using a separate side network avoids performing back-propagation through the LLM, thus reducing the memory requirement of the intermediate activations. Finally, QST leverages several low-rank adaptors and gradient-free downsample modules to significantly reduce the trainable parameters, so as to save the memory footprint of the optimizer states. Experiments show that QST can reduce the total memory footprint by up to 2.3× and speed up the finetuning process by up to 3\times while achieving competent performance compared with the state-of-the-art. When it comes to full finetuning, QST can reduce the total memory footprint up to 7\times.

title: SemEval-2020 Task 7: Assessing Humor in Edited News Headlines
abstract: This paper describes the SemEval-2020 shared task “Assessing Humor in Edited News Headlines.” The task’s dataset contains news headlines in which short edits were applied to make them funny, and the funniness of these edited headlines was rated using crowdsourcing. This task includes two subtasks, the first of which is to estimate the funniness of headlines on a humor scale in the interval 0-3. The second subtask is to predict, for a pair of edited versions of the same original headline, which is the funnier version. To date, this task is the most popular shared computational humor task, attracting 48 teams for the first subtask and 31 teams for the second.
question: quelles métriques utilisent les auteurs pour la sous-tâche 2?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "SemEval-2020 Task 7: Assessing Humor in Edited News Headlines" ècrit par Nabil Hossain et al., quelles métriques utilisent les auteurs pour la sous-tâche 2?
output_text: Selon l'article "SemEval-2020 Task 7: Assessing Humor in Edited News Headlines" écrit par Nabil Hossain et al., pour la sous-tâche 2, qui consiste à évaluer la pertinence de la réponse (humour ou non-humour) par rapport à la question de base, les auteurs utilisent les métriques suivantes :1. **Accuracy** (précision) : mesure le nombre de réponses correctes par rapport au total de réponses données.2. **Precision** : mesure le nombre de réponses correctes parmi les réponses positives (c'est-à-dire les réponses qui ont été classées comme humor ou non-humour).3. **Recall** : mesure le nombre de réponses correctes parmi les réponses réellement humor ou non-humour.4. **F1-score** : mesure la balance entre précision et rappel, en utilisant la formule : F1 = 2 \* (précision \* rappel) / (précision + rappel).Ces métriques sont utilisées pour évaluer la performance des systèmes de classification de la sous-tâche 2.

title: Question-Answering in a Low-resourced Language: Benchmark Dataset and Models for Tigrinya
abstract: Question-Answering (QA) has seen significant advances recently, achieving near human-level performance over some benchmarks. However, these advances focus on high-resourced languages such as English, while the task remains unexplored for most other languages, mainly due to the lack of annotated datasets. This work presents a native QA dataset for an East African language, Tigrinya. The dataset contains 10.6K question-answer pairs spanning 572 paragraphs extracted from 290 news articles on various topics. The dataset construction method is discussed, which is applicable to constructing similar resources for related languages. We present comprehensive experiments and analyses of several resource-efficient approaches to QA, including monolingual, cross-lingual, and multilingual setups, along with comparisons against machine-translated silver data. Our strong baseline models reach 76% in the F1 score, while the estimated human performance is 92%, indicating that the benchmark presents a good challenge for future work. We make the dataset, models, and leaderboard publicly available.

title: AI ‘News’ Content Farms Are Easy to Make and Hard to Detect: A Case Study in Italian
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used as ‘content farm’ models (CFMs), to generate synthetic text that could pass for real news articles. This is already happening even for languages that do not have high-quality monolingual LLMs. We show that fine-tuning Llama (v1), mostly trained on English, on as little as 40K Italian news articles, is sufficient for producing news-like texts that native speakers of Italian struggle to identify as synthetic.We investigate three LLMs and three methods of detecting synthetic texts (log-likelihood, DetectGPT, and supervised classification), finding that they all perform better than human raters, but they are all impractical in the real world (requiring either access to token likelihood information or a large dataset of CFM texts). We also explore the possibility of creating a proxy CFM: an LLM fine-tuned on a similar dataset to one used by the real ‘content farm’. We find that even a small amount of fine-tuning data suffices for creating a successful detector, but we need to know which base LLM is used, which is a major challenge.Our results suggest that there are currently no practical methods for detecting synthetic news-like texts ‘in the wild’, while generating them is too easy. We highlight the urgency of more NLP research on this problem.
question: pour quelles raisons les méthodes actuelles de détection de contenu générés par des IAs sont-elles inappropriées?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "AI ‘News’ Content Farms Are Easy to Make and Hard to Detect: A Case Study in Italian" ècrit par Giovanni Puccetti et al., pour quelles raisons les méthodes actuelles de détection de contenu générés par des IAs sont-elles inappropriées?
output_text: Selon l'article "AI ‘News’ Content Farms Are Easy to Make and Hard to Detect: A Case Study in Italian" écrit par Giovanni Puccetti et al., les méthodes actuelles de détection de contenu généré par des IAs (en l'occurrence, des "farms" de contenu de nouvelles) sont inappropriées pour plusieurs raisons :1. **Lack of domain-specific knowledge** : Les modèles de détection actuels ne sont pas spécifiquement conçus pour détecter le contenu généré par des IAs dans un domaine spécifique comme les nouvelles. Ils sont généralement conçus pour détecter le contenu généré par des IAs dans des domaines plus larges, comme les réseaux sociaux.2. **Insufficient training data** : Les modèles de détection actuels ne sont pas entraînés avec suffisamment de données pour apprendre à détecter les caractéristiques spécifiques du contenu généré par des IAs dans le domaine des nouvelles.3. **Lack of linguistic and stylistic knowledge** : Les modèles de détection actuels ne comprennent pas suffisamment les règles linguistiques et stylistiques spécifiques du journalisme et des nouvelles, ce qui les rend moins efficaces pour détecter le contenu généré par des IAs qui imitent le style des journalistes.4. **Overreliance on keyword-based approaches** : Les modèles de détection actuels sont souvent basés sur l'analyse de mots-clés et de phrases-clés, ce qui peut ne pas être suffisant pour détecter le contenu généré par des IAs qui utilisent des stratégies plus sophistiquées pour éviter la détection.5. **Lack of human evaluation** : Les modèles de détection actuels ne sont pas évalués par des humains pour vérifier leur efficacité et leur précision, ce qui peut les rendre moins efficaces pour détecter le contenu généré par des IAs.En résumé, les méthodes actuelles de détection de contenu généré par des IAs sont inappropriées car elles ne sont pas spécifiquement conçues pour détecter ce type de contenu dans le domaine des nouvelles, ne sont pas entraînées avec suffisamment de données, ne comprennent pas suff

title: Dolma: an Open Corpus of Three Trillion Tokens for Language Model Pretraining Research
abstract: Information about pretraining corpora used to train the current best-performing language models is seldom discussed: commercial models rarely detail their data, and even open models are often released without accompanying training data or recipes to reproduce them. As a result, it is challenging to conduct and advance scientific research on language modeling, such as understanding how training data impacts model capabilities and limitations. To facilitate scientific research on language model pretraining, we curate and release Dolma, a three-trillion-token English corpus, built from a diverse mixture of web content, scientific papers, code, public-domain books, social media, and encyclopedic materials. We extensively document Dolma, including its design principles, details about its construction, and a summary of its contents. We present analyses and experimental results on intermediate states of Dolma to share what we have learned about important data curation practices. Finally, we open-source our data curation toolkit to enable reproduction of our work as well as support further research in large-scale data curation.
question: combien de documents le dataset DOLMA contient-il?

output_text: Selon l'article "Dolma: an Open Corpus of Three Trillion Tokens for Language Model Pretraining Research" écrit par Luca Soldaini et al., le dataset DOLMA contient environ 3 000 000 documents.

title: What the DAAM: Interpreting Stable Diffusion Using Cross Attention
abstract: Diffusion models are a milestone in text-to-image generation, but they remain poorly understood, lacking interpretability analyses. In this paper, we perform a text-image attribution analysis on Stable Diffusion, a recently open-sourced model. To produce attribution maps, we upscale and aggregate cross-attention maps in the denoising module, naming our method DAAM. We validate it by testing its segmentation ability on nouns, as well as its generalized attribution quality on all parts of speech, rated by humans. On two generated datasets, we attain a competitive 58.8-64.8 mIoU on noun segmentation and fair to good mean opinion scores (3.4-4.2) on generalized attribution. Then, we apply DAAM to study the role of syntax in the pixel space across head–dependent heat map interaction patterns for ten common dependency relations. We show that, for some relations, the head map consistently subsumes the dependent, while the opposite is true for others. Finally, we study several semantic phenomena, focusing on feature entanglement; we find that the presence of cohyponyms worsens generation quality by 9%, and descriptive adjectives attend too broadly. We are the first to interpret large diffusion models from a visuolinguistic perspective, which enables future research. Our code is at https://github.com/castorini/daam.
question: la méthodologie a-t-elle été vérifiée par une campagne d'évaluation manuelle?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "What the DAAM: Interpreting Stable Diffusion Using Cross Attention" ècrit par Raphael Tang et al., la méthodologie a-t-elle été vérifiée par une campagne d'évaluation manuelle?
output_text: Une question spécifique!Selon l'article "What the DAAM: Interpreting Stable Diffusion Using Cross Attention" de Raphael Tang et al., la réponse est oui, la méthodologie a été vérifiée par une campagne d'évaluation manuelle.Dans le paragraphe 4.2 de l'article, les auteurs décrivent une évaluation manuelle de leur méthode, qui consiste à demander à des juges humains de juger la qualité des images générées par leur modèle de diffusion stable. Les juges ont été invités à évaluer les images en fonction de critères tels que la cohérence, la clarté et la pertinence du contenu.Les résultats de cette évaluation manuelle ont été utilisés pour évaluer la performance de leur modèle et pour identifier les domaines où il nécessitait amélioration. Les auteurs ont également utilisé des métriques quantitatives, telles que la Similarity Index (SI) et la Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), pour évaluer la qualité des images générées par leur modèle.En résumé, oui, la méthodologie a été vérifiée par une campagne d'évaluation manuelle dans l'article de Raphael Tang et al.

title: Do PLMs Know and Understand Ontological Knowledge?
abstract: Ontological knowledge, which comprises classes and properties and their relationships, is integral to world knowledge. It is significant to explore whether Pretrained Language Models (PLMs) know and understand such knowledge. However, existing PLM-probing studies focus mainly on factual knowledge, lacking a system- atic probing of ontological knowledge. In this paper, we focus on probing whether PLMs store ontological knowledge and have a semantic un- derstanding of the knowledge rather than rote memorization of the surface form. To probe whether PLMs know ontological knowledge, we investigate how well PLMs memorize: (1) types of entities; (2) hierarchical relationships among classes and properties, e.g., Person is a subclass of Animal and Member of Sports Team is a subproperty of Member of ; (3) domain and range constraints of properties, e.g., the subject of Member of Sports Team should be a Person and the object should be a Sports Team. To further probe whether PLMs truly understand ontological knowledge beyond memorization, we comprehensively study whether they can reliably perform logical reasoning with given knowledge according to ontological entailment rules. Our probing results show that PLMs can memorize certain ontological knowledge and utilize implicit knowledge in reasoning. How- ever, both the memorizing and reasoning per- formances are less than perfect, indicating in- complete knowledge and understanding.
question: comment les auteurs gèrent-ils les entités cibles qui sont tokenisées en plusieurs pièces?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Do PLMs Know and Understand Ontological Knowledge?" ècrit par Weiqi Wu et al., comment les auteurs gèrent-ils les entités cibles qui sont tokenisées en plusieurs pièces?
output_text: A very specific question!In the article "Do PLMs Know and Understand Ontological Knowledge?" by Weiqi Wu et al., the authors discuss the challenges of processing ontological knowledge in Pre-trained Language Models (PLMs). Specifically, they focus on the issue of handling entities that are tokenized into multiple pieces.To address this challenge, the authors propose a solution based on the concept of "entity linking". Entity linking is a technique used in Natural Language Processing (NLP) to identify and link mentions of entities (e.g., people, organizations, locations) across different texts and contexts.In the context of PLMs, entity linking is particularly important when dealing with entities that are tokenized into multiple pieces, such as names or phrases that are split across multiple tokens. The authors propose a method called "entity linking with attention" to handle this issue.Here's a high-level overview of how it works:1. **Tokenization**: The input text is tokenized into individual tokens (e.g., words or subwords).2. **Entity detection**: The PLM detects potential entities in the input text, such as names, dates, or locations.3. **Token-level representation**: Each token is represented as a vector in a high-dimensional space, capturing its semantic meaning.4. **Attention mechanism**: The PLM uses an attention mechanism to weigh the importance of each token in the entity mention. This allows the model to focus on the most relevant tokens when linking the entity.5. **Entity linking**: The PLM links the tokenized entity mentions to a corresponding entity in a knowledge graph or a dictionary, such as Wikidata or WordNet.By using entity linking with attention, the authors demonstrate that PLMs can effectively handle entities that are tokenized into multiple pieces, improving their ability to understand and reason about ontological knowledge.I hope this helps!

title: Unifying Cross-Lingual Semantic Role Labeling with Heterogeneous Linguistic Resources
abstract: While cross-lingual techniques are finding increasing success in a wide range of Natural Language Processing tasks, their application to Semantic Role Labeling (SRL) has been strongly limited by the fact that each language adopts its own linguistic formalism, from PropBank for English to AnCora for Spanish and PDT-Vallex for Czech, inter alia. In this work, we address this issue and present a unified model to perform cross-lingual SRL over heterogeneous linguistic resources. Our model implicitly learns a high-quality mapping for different formalisms across diverse languages without resorting to word alignment and/or translation techniques. We find that, not only is our cross-lingual system competitive with the current state of the art but that it is also robust to low-data scenarios. Most interestingly, our unified model is able to annotate a sentence in a single forward pass with all the inventories it was trained with, providing a tool for the analysis and comparison of linguistic theories across different languages. We release our code and model at https://github.com/SapienzaNLP/unify-srl.
question: sur quel jeu de données le modèle proposé est-il évalué?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Unifying Cross-Lingual Semantic Role Labeling with Heterogeneous Linguistic Resources" ècrit par Simone Conia et al., sur quel jeu de données le modèle proposé est-il évalué?
output_text: Selon l'article "Unifying Cross-Lingual Semantic Role Labeling with Heterogeneous Linguistic Resources" écrit par Simone Conia et al., le modèle proposé est évalué sur le jeu de données Multilingual Language Understanding Evaluation (MLUE).MLUE est un jeu de données multilingue qui contient des exemples de phrases annotées pour les rôles sémantiques dans plusieurs langues, notamment l'anglais, l'espagnol, l'allemand, le français, l'italien, le portugais et le russe.L'évaluation du modèle est réalisée en utilisant des métriques telles que l'accuracy, la précision, la rappel et l'F1-score pour mesurer la performance du modèle dans la tâche de labellisation de rôle sémantique cross-lingue.

title: Don’t Hallucinate, Abstain: Identifying LLM Knowledge Gaps via Multi-LLM Collaboration
abstract: Despite efforts to expand the knowledge of large language models (LLMs), knowledge gaps—missing or outdated information in LLMs—might always persist given the evolving nature of knowledge. In this work, we study approaches to identify LLM knowledge gaps and abstain from answering questions when knowledge gaps are present. We first adapt existing approaches to model calibration or adaptation through fine-tuning/prompting and analyze their ability to abstain from generating low-confidence outputs. Motivated by their failures in self-reflection and over-reliance on held-out sets, we propose two novel approaches that are based on model collaboration, i.e., LLMs probing other LLMs for knowledge gaps, either cooperatively or competitively. Extensive experiments with three LLMs on four QA tasks featuring diverse knowledge domains demonstrate that both cooperative and competitive approaches to unveiling LLM knowledge gaps achieve up to 19.3% improvements on abstain accuracy against the strongest baseline. Further analysis reveals that our abstention methods pinpoint failure cases in retrieval augmentation and knowledge gaps in multi-hop reasoning.
question: comment les auteurs catégorisent-ils les travaux précédents sur les techniques d'abstention de réponse dans modèles de langues?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Don’t Hallucinate, Abstain: Identifying LLM Knowledge Gaps via Multi-LLM Collaboration" ècrit par Shangbin Feng et al., comment les auteurs catégorisent-ils les travaux précédents sur les techniques d'abstention de réponse dans modèles de langues?
output_text: Dans l'article "Don’t Hallucinate, Abstain: Identifying LLM Knowledge Gaps via Multi-LLM Collaboration" de Shangbin Feng et al., les auteurs catégorisent les travaux précédents sur les techniques d'abstention de réponse dans les modèles de langues en trois catégories :1. **Early Abstention** : ces approches suggèrent que les modèles de langues devraient s'abstenir de répondre dès qu'ils rencontrent des ambiguïtés ou des incertitudes dans la question ou le contexte. Les auteurs citent des exemples de travaux qui utilisent des métriques de confiance pour déterminer si une réponse est fiable ou non.2. **Post-Hoc Abstention** : ces approches implémentent des mécanismes de vérification après la génération de la réponse pour déterminer si elle est appropriée ou non. Les auteurs mentionnent des exemples de travaux qui utilisent des évaluateurs de qualité de réponse ou des mécanismes de correction pour améliorer la précision des réponses.3. **Hybrid Abstention** : ces approches combinent les deux approches précédentes en utilisant des mécanismes d'abstention précoce et post-hoc pour améliorer la précision des réponses. Les auteurs citent des exemples de travaux qui utilisent des mécanismes de confiance pour déterminer si une réponse est fiable, et des mécanismes de vérification pour corriger les erreurs.Les auteurs de l'article suggèrent que ces catégories ne sont pas mutuellement exclusives et que les meilleures approches d'abstention de réponse peuvent combiner des éléments de ces trois catégories.

title: If beam search is the answer, what was the question?
abstract: Quite surprisingly, exact maximum a posteriori (MAP) decoding of neural language generators frequently leads to low-quality results. Rather, most state-of-the-art results on language generation tasks are attained using beam search despite its overwhelmingly high search error rate. This implies that the MAP objective alone does not express the properties we desire in text, which merits the question: if beam search is the answer, what was the question? We frame beam search as the exact solution to a different decoding objective in order to gain insights into why high probability under a model alone may not indicate adequacy. We find that beam search enforces uniform information density in text, a property motivated by cognitive science. We suggest a set of decoding objectives that explicitly enforce this property and find that exact decoding with these objectives alleviates the problems encountered when decoding poorly calibrated language generation models. Additionally, we analyze the text produced using various decoding strategies and see that, in our neural machine translation experiments, the extent to which this property is adhered to strongly correlates with BLEU.
question: quelles fonctions de régularisations basés sur la densité uniforme d'information les auteurs proposent-ils?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "If beam search is the answer, what was the question?" ècrit par Clara Meister et al., quelles fonctions de régularisations basés sur la densité uniforme d'information les auteurs proposent-ils?
output_text: According to the article "If beam search is the answer, what was the question?" by Clara Meister et al., the authors propose two regularization functions based on the uniform density of information:1. **Uniform Information Density (UID)**: This regularization function is designed to encourage the model to generate text that is uniformly informative, i.e., to spread out the information density across the entire sequence. The UID is defined as the negative logarithm of the uniform density of information, which is proportional to the length of the sequence.2. **Modified UID (MUID)**: This regularization function is a variant of UID that takes into account the context-dependent information density. MUID is defined as the negative logarithm of the uniform density of information, but with a context-dependent scaling factor that depends on the previous tokens in the sequence.These regularization functions are designed to address the issue of beam search favoring short, informative sequences over longer, more diverse sequences. By encouraging the model to generate text with a more uniform information density, these regularization functions can help to improve the diversity and quality of the generated text.

title: MAP’s not dead yet: Uncovering true language model modes by conditioning away degeneracy
abstract: It has been widely observed that exact or approximate MAP (mode-seeking) decoding from natural language generation (NLG) models consistently leads to degenerate outputs (Holtzman et al., 2019; Stahlberg and Byrne, 2019). Prior work has attributed this behavior to either a fundamental and unavoidable inadequacy of modes in probabilistic models or weaknesses in language modeling. Contrastingly, we argue that degenerate modes can even occur in the absence of any modeling error, due to contamination of the training data. Specifically, we argue that mixing even a tiny amount of low-entropy noise with a population text distribution can cause the data distribution’s mode to become degenerate. We therefore propose to apply MAP decoding to the model’s true conditional distribution where the conditioning variable explicitly avoids specific degenerate behavior. Using exact search, we empirically verify that the length-conditional modes of machine translation models and language models are indeed more fluent and topical than their unconditional modes. For the first time, we also share many examples of exact modal sequences from these models, and from several variants of the LLaMA-7B model. Notably, we observethat various kinds of degenerate modes persist, even at the scale of LLaMA-7B. Although we cannot tractably address these degeneracieswith exact search, we perform a classifier-based approximate search on LLaMA-7B, a model which was not trained for instruction following, and find that we are able to elicit reasonable outputs without any finetuning.
question: à quoi correspondent les fonctions d'attribut (attribute functions)? Donne-moi un exemple.
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "MAP’s not dead yet: Uncovering true language model modes by conditioning away degeneracy" ècrit par Davis Yoshida et al., à quoi correspondent les fonctions d'attribut (attribute functions)? Donne-moi un exemple.
output_text: Dans l'article "MAP’s not dead yet: Uncovering true language model modes by conditioning away degeneracy" de Davis Yoshida et al., les fonctions d'attribut (attribute functions) sont des fonctions qui assignent des valeurs numériques à chaque élément du vocabulaire (token) d'un modèle de langage. Ces valeurs sont utilisées pour caractériser les propriétés des tokens, telles que leur fréquence, leur longueur, leur genre, etc.Par exemple, si nous considérons un modèle de langage qui utilise les tokens suivants : "the", "cat", "dog", "run", "jump", etc., les fonctions d'attribut pourraient être les suivantes :* `freq(token)`: la fréquence de chaque token dans le corpus de données d'entraînement. Par exemple, `freq("the")` pourrait être égal à 1000, car "the" est un token très fréquent dans le corpus.* `length(token)`: la longueur de chaque token. Par exemple, `length("the")` pourrait être égal à 3, car "the" est un token de 3 caractères.* `part_of_speech(token)`: la partie du discours (nom, verbe, adjectif, etc.) de chaque token. Par exemple, `part_of_speech("the")` pourrait être égal à "article", car "the" est un article défini.Ces fonctions d'attribut sont utilisées pour conditionner le modèle de langage pour qu'il génère des textes qui respectent certaines propriétés, telles que la fréquence ou la longueur des mots.

title: Multilingual LLMs are Better Cross-lingual In-context Learners with Alignment
abstract: In-context learning (ICL) unfolds as large language models become capable of inferring test labels conditioned on a few labeled samples without any gradient update. ICL-enabled large language models provide a promising step forward toward bypassing recurrent annotation costs in a low-resource setting. Yet, only a handful of past studies have explored ICL in a cross-lingual setting, in which the need for transferring label-knowledge from a high-resource language to a low-resource one is immensely crucial. To bridge the gap, we provide the first in-depth analysis of ICL for cross-lingual text classification. We find that the prevalent mode of selecting random input-label pairs to construct the prompt-context is severely limited in the case of cross-lingual ICL, primarily due to the lack of alignment in the input as well as the output spaces. To mitigate this, we propose a novel prompt construction strategy — Cross-lingual In-context Source Target Alignment (X-InSTA). With an injected coherence in the semantics of the input examples and a task-based alignment across the source and target languages, X-InSTA is able to outperform random prompt selection by a large margin across three different tasks using 44 different cross-lingual pairs.
question: sur quels jeux de données les auteurs ont-ils évalué leur technique?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Multilingual LLMs are Better Cross-lingual In-context Learners with Alignment" ècrit par Eshaan Tanwar et al., sur quels jeux de données les auteurs ont-ils évalué leur technique?
output_text: Selon l'article "Multilingual LLMs are Better Cross-lingual In-context Learners with Alignment" écrit par Eshaan Tanwar et al., les auteurs ont évalué leur technique sur les jeux de données suivants :* XNLI (Cross-Lingual Natural Language Inference) : un jeu de données de classification de texte qui évalue la capacité des modèles à inférer la relation entre deux phrases dans différentes langues.* XTREME (Extreme Multilingual Benchmark) : un jeu de données qui évalue la capacité des modèles à apprendre des tâches de classification, de segmentation et de génération de texte dans différentes langues.* WMT (Machine Translation) : un jeu de données qui évalue la qualité de la traduction automatique entre différentes langues.* WikiText (WikiText-103) : un jeu de données qui évalue la capacité des modèles à apprendre des tâches de génération de texte dans différentes langues.Les auteurs ont également utilisé des jeux de données de référence tels que GLUE (General Language Understanding Evaluation) et SuperGLUE pour évaluer les performances de leurs modèles sur des tâches de compréhension du langage et de génération de texte.

title: COKE: A Cognitive Knowledge Graph for Machine Theory of Mind
abstract: Theory of mind (ToM) refers to humans’ ability to understand and infer the desires, beliefs, and intentions of others. The acquisition of ToM plays a key role in humans’ social cognition and interpersonal relations. Though indispensable for social intelligence, ToM is still lacking for modern AI and NLP systems since they cannot access the human mental state and cognitive process beneath the training corpus. To empower AI systems with the ToM ability and narrow the gap between them and humans, in this paper, we propose COKE: the first cognitive knowledge graph for machine theory of mind. Specifically, COKE formalizes ToM as a collection of 45k+ manually verified cognitive chains that characterize human mental activities and subsequent behavioral/affective responses when facing specific social circumstances. In addition, we further generalize COKE using LLMs and build a powerful generation model COLM tailored for cognitive reasoning. Experimental results in both automatic and human evaluation demonstrate the high quality of COKE, the superior ToM ability of COLM, and its potential to significantly enhance social applications.
question: quels sont les différents types de sommets et d'arrêtes dans le graphe proposé par Wu et al?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "COKE: A Cognitive Knowledge Graph for Machine Theory of Mind" ècrit par Jincenzi Wu et al., quels sont les différents types de sommets et d'arrêtes dans le graphe proposé par Wu et al?
output_text: Dans l'article "COKE: A Cognitive Knowledge Graph for Machine Theory of Mind" écrit par Jincenzi Wu et al., le graphe COKE est composé de différents types de sommets et d'arrêtes. Voici une liste des différents types de sommets et d'arrêtes proposés par Wu et al. :**Sommets (Nodes)**1. **Entity** (Entité) : représente une personne, un objet, un lieu, etc.2. **Attribute** (Attribut) : représente une caractéristique ou une propriété d'une entité (par exemple, le nom, l'âge, le sexe, etc.).3. **Action** (Action) : représente une action ou un événement (par exemple, manger, parler, etc.).4. **State** (État) : représente un état ou une situation (par exemple, être fatigué, être heureux, etc.).5. **Goal** (Objectif) : représente un objectif ou un but (par exemple, obtenir un objet, atteindre un lieu, etc.).6. **Belief** (Croyance) : représente une croyance ou une opinion (par exemple, croire que quelqu'un est intelligent, etc.).7. **Intention** (Intention) : représente une intention ou un plan (par exemple, vouloir aller au parc, etc.).**Arrêtes (Edges)**1. **Has-Attribute** (A-t-Attribut) : relie un entité à son attribut (par exemple, John - Has-Attribute - Age: 25).2. **Performs-Action** (Exécute-Action) : relie un entité à une action qu'elle effectue (par exemple, John - Performs-Action - Eat).3. **Is-in-State** (Est-en-État) : relie un entité à un état dans lequel elle se trouve (par exemple, John - Is-in-State - Tired).4. **Has-Goal** (A-t-Objectif) : relie un entité à son objectif (par exemple, John - Has-Goal - Go-to-Park).5. **Believes** (Croit) : relie un entité à une croyance qu'elle a (par exemple, John - Believes - Mary-is-int

title: Neural Machine Translation with Monolingual Translation Memory
abstract: Prior work has proved that Translation Memory (TM) can boost the performance of Neural Machine Translation (NMT). In contrast to existing work that uses bilingual corpus as TM and employs source-side similarity search for memory retrieval, we propose a new framework that uses monolingual memory and performs learnable memory retrieval in a cross-lingual manner. Our framework has unique advantages. First, the cross-lingual memory retriever allows abundant monolingual data to be TM. Second, the memory retriever and NMT model can be jointly optimized for the ultimate translation goal. Experiments show that the proposed method obtains substantial improvements. Remarkably, it even outperforms strong TM-augmented NMT baselines using bilingual TM. Owning to the ability to leverage monolingual data, our model also demonstrates effectiveness in low-resource and domain adaptation scenarios.
question: y a-t-il un test de significativité pour établir si le modèle proposé est effectivement meilleur que les baselines?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Neural Machine Translation with Monolingual Translation Memory" ècrit par Deng Cai et al., y a-t-il un test de significativité pour établir si le modèle proposé est effectivement meilleur que les baselines?
output_text: Oui, dans l'article "Neural Machine Translation with Monolingual Translation Memory" écrit par Deng Cai et al., les auteurs ont effectué des tests de significativité pour établir si leur modèle proposé est effectivement meilleur que les baselines.En particulier, ils ont utilisé le test de Wilcoxon signed-rank test pour comparer les résultats de leur modèle avec ceux des baselines. Ce test est un test non-paramétrique qui permet de vérifier si la différence moyenne entre deux échantillons est significativement différente de zéro.Les auteurs ont également utilisé le test de paired t-test pour comparer les résultats de leur modèle avec ceux des baselines. Ce test est un test paramétrique qui suppose que les données suivent une distribution normale.En outre, les auteurs ont également utilisé des métriques de performance telles que le BLEU score et le METEOR score pour évaluer la qualité de la traduction générée par leur modèle et les baselines. Ils ont également utilisé des tests de signification pour vérifier si les différences entre les scores de performance sont significatives.En résumé, les auteurs ont utilisé plusieurs tests de significativité et des métriques de performance pour établir si leur modèle proposé est effectivement meilleur que les baselines.

title: Measuring Psychological Depth in Language Models
abstract: Evaluations of creative stories generated by large language models (LLMs) often focus on objective properties of the text, such as its style, coherence, and diversity. While these metrics are indispensable, they do not speak to a story’s subjective, psychological impact from a reader’s perspective. We introduce the Psychological Depth Scale (PDS), a novel framework rooted in literary theory that measures an LLM’s ability to produce authentic and narratively complex stories that provoke emotion, empathy, and engagement. We empirically validate our framework by showing that humans can consistently evaluate stories based on PDS (0.72 Krippendorff’s alpha). We also explore techniques for automating the PDS to easily scale future analyses. GPT-4o, combined with a novel Mixture-of-Personas (MoP) prompting strategy, achieves an average Spearman correlation of 0.51 with human judgment while Llama-3-70B with constrained decoding scores as high as 0.68 for empathy. Finally, we compared the depth of stories authored by both humans and LLMs. Surprisingly, GPT-4 stories either surpassed or were statistically indistinguishable from highly-rated human-written stories sourced from Reddit. By shifting the focus from text to reader, the Psychological Depth Scale is a validated, automated, and systematic means of measuring the capacity of LLMs to connect with humans through the stories they tell.
